articles.title: Scrying the AMD GFX1250 LLVM Tea Leaves
articles.summary: AMD's upcoming MI400 series of Datacenter Accelerators, codenamed GFX1250 and GFX1251, are expected to offer significant improvements over their predecessors. The new accelerators will power the Helios rack and deliver native double-precision compute capabilities projected to be around 200 TFLOPs. A key feature is the WGP (Workgroup Processor) architecture, which combines two pairs of SIMD32 arrays into a single unit, similar to consumer architectures but with some differences in caching hierarchy. This change may not have significant structural implications for the compiler, as it simply allows for shared vector L0 cache across the whole workgroup processor.

articles.title: Software and AI - Plotting vs. Pantsing
articles.summary: Software engineers use two approaches: plotting and pantsing. Plotting involves developing a solution before writing code, emphasizing top-down analytical construction, whereas pantsing focuses on creating from bottom-up synthesis, treating code as an expression of design. The difference lies in how code is treated and where understanding lives, with plotting emphasizing the design and pantsing focusing on the code itself. This distinction can help explain why AI usage differs in ease and usefulness, with plotting approaches potentially leading to more autonomous AI updates and assistants supporting plotting.

articles.title: Studying Linux Schedulers, and Why Metrics Matter
articles.summary: Studying Linux Schedulers, and Why Metrics Matter A cruel reminder that measuring stuff is hard Last semester's Parallel Computer Architectures term project involved investigating how much scheduling decisions matter in the Linux Scheduler, specifically on dual-socket Xeon systems. The scheduler's classical advice to keep processes on the same core for as long as possible is often overlooked, as it doesn't account for minimizing cache hierarchy traversals when shared memory accesses occur. This can lead to inefficient use of compute resources and increased coherence actions, particularly in multi-threaded programs.

articles.title: neither gcc nor clang are compliant with standard c++
articles.summary: Neither GCC nor Clang are compliant with standard C++ 2026-07-08 when it comes to function types, which have a "language linkage" associated with them. This means that two function types with different language linkages are distinct even if they are otherwise identical. The standard explicitly states this and prohibits storing language linkage information with the type. The problem lies not with GCC or Clang, but with the standard itself, as it is currently incorrect to allow for such implementation-defined behavior.

articles.title: Regressive JPEGs
articles.summary: Regressive JPEGs can be used to save low frequency components first in the file, allowing for partial downloads at lower resolution. This feature works by breaking up compressed data into multiple scans, each prefixed with a header that includes information about the scan's spectral range and precision. The resulting concatenated image can then be served over a slow network without causing decoding issues due to the presence of start-of-image, start-of-frame, and end-of-image markers.
